impacts.caseName: Teva Pharmaceuticals International GmbH v. Eli Lilly and Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court erred in granting judgment as a matter of law that Teva's headache patents claiming methods of using humanized anti-CGRP antagonist antibodies to treat headache were invalid for failure to satisfy the written-description and enablement requirements of 35 U.S.C. § 112.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's JMOL of invalidity, reinstating a jury verdict that Lilly willfully infringed Teva's headache patents covering methods of treating headache with humanized anti-CGRP antagonist antibodies. This is a high-stakes pharmaceutical patent dispute involving blockbuster migraine treatments.
impacts.positiveImplications: Strengthens patent holders' ability to defend genus claims in the pharmaceutical/biologics space where the specification discloses one species and references known methods for making others, reinforcing protection for valuable biologic drug patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Businesses developing competing biologics face greater risk that broad method-of-treatment claims covering a genus of antibodies will survive validity challenges, potentially limiting market entry for biosimilars and follow-on biologics.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder that conditions patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products commits unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when the tied products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act for conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the jury's finding that the honeycomb products had substantial non-infringing uses beyond the patent's scope, defeating Ingevity's patent-misuse defense.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors benefit from confirmation that patent holders cannot leverage patent rights to compel exclusive purchases of unpatented products when those products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust liability risk when structuring licensing arrangements that require customers to purchase unpatented products, even in markets where the patent covers only some uses of those products.

impacts.caseName: The Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods for detecting anomalous program executions using an emulator and a model of function calls are directed to abstract ideas under step one of the Alice framework, and whether damages for foreign sales of infringing software are recoverable.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that the asserted cybersecurity patents were abstract at Alice step one and remanding for step two analysis. The court also ruled that damages based on Norton's foreign software sales were improperly awarded, and reversed the contempt order against Norton's counsel Quinn Emanuel that had supported enhanced damages.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies benefit from reinforced § 101 eligibility scrutiny for software patents claiming detection methods using models and comparisons, and from limits on recovering damages for foreign sales.
impacts.negativeImplications: Universities and other patent holders face heightened risk that cybersecurity and software detection patents will be found patent-ineligible, and cannot recover damages based on foreign sales of accused software products.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming server-side methods for tailoring content presentation specifications to a wireless device's capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's streaming patents claiming methods of tailoring content to device capabilities are ineligible under § 101, reversing the district court's judgment in GoTV's favor on the '715 patent and a $2.5 million damages award. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of adapting content for device display.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and technology companies gain a strong precedent for invalidating patents on content adaptation and device-tailored delivery under § 101, reducing litigation exposure.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to server-side content optimization and adaptive streaming face increased vulnerability to § 101 challenges.

impacts.caseName: VLSI Technology LLC v. Intel Corporation
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder can recover damages for infringement of method claims where the accused multicore processor chips are designed domestically but predominantly manufactured and sold abroad, and whether doctrine of equivalents was properly applied.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed in part the district court's grant of summary judgment of noninfringement to Intel on VLSI's '836 patent relating to multicore processor task allocation, finding that the extraterritoriality ruling was in error but affirming on the doctrine of equivalents ground. The court also reversed the striking of VLSI's damages expert theories.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the court's rejection of broad extraterritoriality defenses where significant infringing activity occurs domestically, preserving the ability to assert method patents against global chip manufacturers.
impacts.negativeImplications: Semiconductor and technology companies with global manufacturing operations face continued exposure to U.S. patent infringement claims even when manufacturing predominantly occurs overseas.

impacts.caseName: International Medical Devices, Inc. v. Cornell
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the plaintiff presented legally sufficient evidence that each of its four asserted trade secrets constituted protectable trade secrets under California law, and whether ideas that are generally known can constitute an inventive contribution for patent inventorship purposes.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the denial of JMOL on trade secret misappropriation claims, finding insufficient evidence that the asserted trade secrets were protectable under California law, and vacated the reasonable royalty, exemplary damages, and permanent injunction. The court also reversed on patent inventorship, holding that generally known ideas cannot constitute inventive contributions.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses accused of trade secret misappropriation benefit from heightened scrutiny of whether asserted trade secrets are truly protectable, particularly when the claimed information overlaps with generally known concepts.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trade secret holders face a higher evidentiary bar to establish protectability, and cannot rely on generally known information to support both trade secret and inventorship claims simultaneously.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in patent prosecution and on a Walker Process fraud claim for attempted monopolization based on enforcement of a fraudulently obtained patent.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment of inequitable conduct against Tenaris and summary judgment in Tenaris's favor on Global Tubing's Walker Process antitrust claim, finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. The case involves patents on coiled tubing for oil and gas wells where the patentee allegedly failed to disclose prior art acquired from a predecessor company.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the court's insistence on a full factual record before finding inequitable conduct, rather than resolving such fact-intensive questions on summary judgment.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies acquiring patents through asset purchases face renewed litigation risk where prior art from predecessor companies was not disclosed during prosecution, including potential Walker Process antitrust liability.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether Rule 30(b)(6) corporate representative testimony is admissible at summary judgment notwithstanding the witness's lack of personal knowledge, and whether the on-sale bar of pre-AIA 35 U.S.C. § 102(b) requires that prior art sales expressly disclose the device's functionality.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed summary judgment invalidating the '689 patent under the on-sale bar, holding that 30(b)(6) corporate representative testimony is admissible even absent personal knowledge and rejecting the argument that the on-sale bar requires express disclosure of a device's internal functionality at the time of sale.
impacts.positiveImplications: Accused infringers can rely on corporate representative depositions and historical sales evidence to establish the on-sale bar, even when original employees are no longer available.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders cannot defeat on-sale bar challenges by arguing that prior commercial sales failed to expressly disclose the internal functionality of the sold product, broadening the scope of invalidating prior art.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple Watch models incorporating blood oxygen measurement functionality infringed Masimo's patents and whether Masimo satisfied the domestic industry requirement.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated § 337 by importing Apple Watch models infringing Masimo's blood oxygenation patents, upholding the limited exclusion order barring importation. The court found no error in the domestic industry determination, validity rulings, or infringement findings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in wearable health technology are affirmed in their ability to obtain ITC exclusion orders against major technology companies importing infringing products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies incorporating health monitoring features into consumer devices face significant risk of ITC exclusion orders that can disrupt product availability and supply chains.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent may obtain a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) of a refiled declaratory judgment action when it previously missed the deadline for seeking a stay of the original action involving the same parties and issues.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that an ITC respondent cannot circumvent the statutory deadline for seeking a mandatory stay of a district court action by voluntarily dismissing and refiling the action. BioMarin could not obtain a mandatory stay of Ascendis's refiled declaratory judgment action after missing the 30-day deadline in the original action.
impacts.positiveImplications: Declaratory judgment plaintiffs in pharmaceutical patent disputes are protected from ITC respondents using tactical refiling to obtain mandatory stays and delay resolution of safe-harbor defenses.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents lose the ability to strategically refile district court actions to reset mandatory stay deadlines, limiting their ability to coordinate parallel ITC and district court proceedings.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTAB erred in its construction of claim terms in a patent covering systems and methods for streaming partly encrypted media content, leading to an incorrect obviousness determination.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the PTAB's claim construction in an IPR challenging DivX's streaming encryption patent, vacated the Board's decision rejecting Netflix's obviousness challenge, and remanded for further proceedings. The decision impacts how encryption-related claim terms in streaming media patents are construed.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming companies benefit from broader claim constructions that increase the likelihood of invalidating overly broad streaming media patents through IPR proceedings.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the streaming and digital media encryption space face risk that narrower claim constructions will be rejected, potentially exposing their patents to prior art challenges.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding the patent holder's damages expert report and granting summary judgment of no remedy based on that exclusion.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report in a patent infringement case against Microsoft's Azure platform, vacating the summary judgment based on absence of a remedy. The court found the district court applied too rigid a standard in evaluating the expert's methodology.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from a more flexible standard for admitting damages expert reports, reducing the risk that technical deficiencies in expert methodology will result in case-dispositive exclusion.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers like major cloud platform providers lose an important early-stage defense tool, as courts will be less likely to exclude damages experts and grant summary judgment on that basis alone.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the PTAB governing discretionary denial of IPR institution are 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions to the Board on how to exercise discretion in declining IPR institution are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking. The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution and that the Director retains ultimate authority over non-institution decisions.
impacts.positiveImplications: The PTO retains broad flexibility to issue and modify guidance on discretionary IPR denial without formal rulemaking, allowing responsive adjustments to patent quality mechanisms.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies relying on IPR as a key tool to challenge patents face continued uncertainty, as the PTO can alter institution practices through informal policy statements without public input or transparency requirements.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users and whether incentive awards to nonprofit class representatives were permissible.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's approval of a $125 million nationwide class action settlement resolving claims that the federal judiciary charged excessive PACER fees to fund unrelated expenses. The court upheld attorneys' fees awards and incentive awards to nonprofit organizational class representatives, finding no abuse of discretion.
impacts.positiveImplications: The settlement provides substantial reimbursement to hundreds of thousands of PACER users, including law firms and businesses that routinely access federal court records, and validates incentive awards to organizational class representatives.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether means-plus-function claim limitations in a patent for crop harvester height-control apparatus were indefinite where the specification disclosed a conventional controller without detailed structural disclosure.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that claims of the '395 patent were invalid as indefinite, holding that the specification's disclosure of a conventional controller, head controller, and hydraulic control system provided sufficient corresponding structure for the means-plus-function limitations. The case is remanded for further proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with means-plus-function claims benefit from the holding that disclosure of conventional components as corresponding structure can satisfy definiteness requirements, preserving enforceability of mechanical and agricultural patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers like Deere face continued patent enforcement where they had expected indefiniteness to invalidate means-plus-function claims with general structural disclosures.
